data.description:
Thinking about what to do with Fluor stock right now? You’re not alone. Whenever a stock posts some pretty sharp ups and downs, it gets everyone’s attention, and Fluor has definitely kept things interesting. After a bumpy week with the price dropping 8.4%, it’s easy to wonder if there’s more turbulence ahead or if these dips are opportunities in disguise. Despite that recent slide, Fluor is still up 3.8% over the past month, but down 5.7% for the year and 12.1% over the last 12 months, hinting at some short-term uncertainty. But zoom out and the picture gets very different. The stock has soared an impressive 327.7% over five years, showing serious long-term growth potential.

This mixed performance isn’t happening in a vacuum. Fluor has made headlines recently as it’s expanded into new project markets and emphasized sustainability in its operations. These trends could shift how investors look at the company’s value. With a value score of 4 out of 6, Fluor currently looks undervalued in several important ways, catching the eye of many value-focused investors.

Approach 1: Fluor Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s intrinsic value by projecting its future free cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value to account for the time value of money. This approach essentially tries to answer how much today’s investor should pay for all the future cash Fluor is expected to generate.

For Fluor, the latest twelve-month free cash flow stands at $228 million. Analyst forecasts see this expanding quickly, with projections reaching $540 million by the end of 2029. Estimates beyond five years are derived by extrapolating trends identified by Simply Wall St, as direct analyst figures become scarce. Over the next decade, the model expects cash flows to keep rising, though at a slowing pace, reflecting a company that is becoming more stable as it grows.

Approach 2: Fluor Price vs Earnings (P/E Ratio)

For companies like Fluor that are generating profits, the Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio is a key tool in the valuation kit. The P/E ratio lets investors see how much they're paying for each dollar of earnings. A lower P/E can mean the market expects little growth or sees higher risk in the company, while a higher P/E often reflects strong future growth expectations or lower perceived risk.

As it stands, Fluor’s current P/E ratio is just 1.8x, which is dramatically lower than the Construction industry average of 32.6x and also far below the peer average of 30.3x. This huge gap immediately suggests that the market is pricing in some caution or uncertainty about near-term prospects.

With Fluor trading at a much lower P/E than both its Fair Ratio and the broader market, the stock currently looks significantly undervalued based on this metric, although the very low P/E may be factoring in some unspecific risks that investors should be aware of.

Many enterprises, and even government agencies, continue to double down on cyber defense. This trend plays right into CrowdStrike’s hands. News of big customer wins and the company’s expansion into new international markets have reinforced the story of long-term growth potential, even as risk perceptions shift in response to threats and innovation across the sector. This approach helps investors gauge what the company's underlying cash generation is worth today, separate from market hype.

The "right" P/S ratio often depends on growth prospects and industry risk. High-growth companies or those with a significant competitive edge usually command higher P/S multiples. data.title: Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB): Margin Decline Reinforces Concerns About Profit Durability
data.description:
Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB) reported current net profit margins of 26.9%, down from 28.3% a year ago, signaling a decline in profitability year over year. Over the past five years, earnings have grown at 5.6% annually, with revenue projected to rise 7.3% per year going forward. This is slower than the broader US market. However, forecast earnings growth of 19.7% per year outpaces national averages, even after a dip in earnings over the last twelve months. Investors are weighing the company’s attractive dividend, profit growth, and valuation metrics against the backdrop of ongoing margin compression and slower revenue expansion compared to peers.

  • What’s surprising is that, even with a higher-than-industry Price-to-Earnings ratio of 12.8x (compared to 11.2x for US Banks), the current price remains well below DCF fair value. This strongly supports the investment case that the market may be under-appreciating the company's ability to generate high-quality earnings.
    • This valuation gap suggests the market could be assigning a premium for quality, but it is not fully pricing in future earnings growth, which is forecast at 19.7% per year and is faster than the US average.
    • Despite a slower revenue growth outlook compared to the overall market, the deep discount to fair value provides meaningful upside for long-term investors willing to look past short-term margin pressure.

Margins Slip as Profit Quality Remains High

  • Net profit margins stand at 26.9%, down from 28.3% last year. This represents a decrease of 1.4 percentage points, but the company is still recognized for high-quality earnings.
  • The data highlights a key tension: while profit margins are tightening, the company’s blend of ongoing profitability and above-average earnings growth contrasts with the cautious narrative that shrinking margins should threaten bottom line durability.
    • With earnings per year projected to rise 19.7%, management is still delivering substantial profit, which strongly supports the case that Bar Harbor’s profitability is more defensible than typical regional banks.
    • In the absence of major risk statements, the sustained quality of earnings stands out as a reason bullish investors remain patient despite margin compression.

Trading Rich to Peers but Still Labeled “Good Value”

  • The company’s Price-To-Earnings ratio of 12.8x places it above both its peer average of 12.5x and the broader US Banks industry at 11.2x, underscoring its premium valuation.

data.title: Lockheed Martin (LMT) Margin Decline Challenges Bullish Narratives Despite Strong Valuation and Earnings Quality
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) posted a net profit margin of 5.7%, down from 9.4% last year, with average annual earnings slipping 4.6% over the past five years. While the recent trend shows slower growth, forecasts call for annual profit growth of 14.3% and revenue growth of 3.2% per year, both lagging the broader US market. Despite this, the company’s earnings quality remains high, and the stock stands out for its attractive valuation, solid dividend, and continued growth expectations, helping to keep investor sentiment steady.

See our full analysis for Lockheed Martin.

Now, let's see how the latest results measure up against the most widely held narratives for Lockheed Martin. Where do the numbers back up the market story, and where do they challenge it?

See what the community is saying about Lockheed Martin

Advertisement

Profit Margin Slide Reflects Legacy Program Strain

  • Lockheed Martin’s net profit margin dropped to 5.7%, a clear contraction from 9.4% a year ago, largely due to persistent cost overruns and integration challenges in major legacy programs.
  • Analysts' consensus view points to how margin pressures are set against a backdrop of ongoing operational improvements and enhanced risk controls.
    • Contract discipline and digital transformation initiatives are expected to support margin recovery, but legacy program charges, such as the $1.8 billion hit expected in Q2 2025, threaten near-term profitability.
    • This struggle aligns with consensus concerns that further profit compression could occur if technical hurdles and budget risks persist across Lockheed’s core classified and helicopter programs.
  • Analysts see Lockheed’s push for efficiency as potentially turning the tide, but only if cost discipline sticks.
    • Rising U.S. munitions spending and multi-year contracts could stabilize margins, but overexposure to large legacy projects still creates headwinds in the short term.
    • Revenue growth visibility remains robust, yet improving profits depends critically on avoiding further cost overruns, especially in fixed-price contracts.

Valuation Advantage Versus Peers Holds Up

  • The stock trades at a lower price-to-earnings ratio than its industry and peer average, while also sitting significantly below DCF fair value ($487.14 per share compared to a DCF fair value of $575.19), indicating analysts see Lockheed Martin as attractively priced given expected earnings growth.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative argues that Lockheed Martin’s fair pricing rests on forecasts for rising profit margins, from 5.9% today to 8.7% in three years, and revenue expected to reach $81 billion by 2028.
    • The consensus price target of $520.41 sits just above the current share price, suggesting the market mostly agrees with these optimistic forecasts, but there is debate: some analysts project much higher or lower targets depending on how cost and contract risks play out.
    • Consensus weighs Lockheed’s attractive PE discount and fair pricing against a backdrop of budget uncertainty and heightened competition, leaving the door open for re-rating if margins do improve.
  • On valuation metrics, Lockheed’s PE ratio is not only below the US Aerospace & Defense average (currently 34.4x) but also expected to compress further to 18.9x by 2028 if projections materialize.
    • A relatively small 4.1% upside to the consensus price target implies much of the good news may already be priced in unless earnings or margins outpace current analyst expectations.
    • The gap between today’s price and DCF fair value offers a cushion, especially if Lockheed can deliver on backlog and technology-driven margin expansion.

Backlog and R&D Investment Underpin Long-Term Case

  • Analyst consensus highlights robust demand for advanced platforms like the F-35, PAC-3, and hypersonic weapons, fueling sustained backlog and providing strong visibility for revenue and cash flow stability across multiple years.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative notes that long-term revenue and earnings durability rely on continued investment in next-generation technologies, such as hypersonics and missile defense.

data.title: Is CoreWeave’s Recent 12.7% Dip a Sign of Opportunity in 2025?
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with CoreWeave stock? You're not alone. After a jaw-dropping 203.8% surge year-to-date, it’s fair to say CoreWeave has grabbed the attention of both seasoned investors and first-timers trying to catch the next big wave. But even the hottest stocks take a breather, and CoreWeave’s recent dip, dropping 12.7% in the past week and 8.8% over the last month, has made a lot of people wonder if this is a golden buying opportunity or a sign of more turbulence ahead.

Much of this price whiplash can be traced back to recent headlines. There’s been plenty of buzz about CoreWeave expanding its data center capabilities and forming new partnerships, signaling commitment to long-term growth. As a result, some investors are viewing the current volatility as temporary, possibly linked to shifting market risk sentiment, rather than a fundamental change in the company’s prospects. Meanwhile, with a value score of 4 out of 6 based on our six-point undervaluation check, there’s a strong case that CoreWeave remains attractively valued for those who know what to look for.

So, how do you cut through the noise and figure out what CoreWeave is truly worth? Let’s dig into the nuts and bolts of its current valuation, from traditional comparisons to more forward-thinking models. At the end, I’ll share a smarter way to judge CoreWeave’s real potential before making your next move.

CoreWeave delivered 0.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the IT industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: CoreWeave Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s value by projecting future cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s dollars. This approach is widely used for tech companies like CoreWeave because it focuses on how much cash the business is expected to generate over time.

As of the latest report, CoreWeave’s Free Cash Flow (FCF) stands at -$11.1 million, reflecting the company’s current phase of heavy investment and rapid expansion. Looking forward, analyst estimates expect CoreWeave’s FCF to turn positive and keep accelerating, with projections forecast at $5.7 million by 2029. Further out, Simply Wall St provides an extrapolated ten-year projection of $27.9 million by 2035, which illustrates robust optimism for sustained growth.

With these numbers, the DCF model estimates CoreWeave’s fair value at $431.58. This implies the stock is trading at a steep discount of 71.8 percent. In this scenario, CoreWeave is seen as significantly undervalued at current prices, based purely on the future cash it is expected to generate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for CoreWeave.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests CoreWeave is undervalued by 71.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: CoreWeave Price vs Sales

The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is widely used to value growth companies that are either unprofitable or in the early stages of generating significant earnings. For businesses like CoreWeave, which are investing heavily in future growth and may not yet be turning a profit, the P/S ratio provides a useful apples-to-apples measure of how much investors are paying for each dollar of revenue.

Of course, not all P/S ratios are created equal. Rapidly growing companies or those with unique market opportunities often command a higher P/S, but this premium should be justified by the company’s prospects and risk profile. Industry averages and peer multiples set the baseline, but factors like growth rates, margins, and business risks can all push the "normal" valuation higher or lower.

Right now, CoreWeave is trading at a P/S ratio of 17.87x, which is below its peer group’s average P/S of 21.39x, but far above the broader IT industry average of 2.78x. While this might raise eyebrows at first glance, Simply Wall St’s proprietary Fair Ratio for CoreWeave is calculated at 45.55x. This Fair Ratio is tailored to CoreWeave’s specific circumstances, taking into account not just its sales but also factors like growth trajectory, profitability outlook, market cap, and unique risks. Compared to a plain benchmark or industry average, the Fair Ratio offers a more holistic and forward-looking view of what is truly “fair” for CoreWeave’s valuation.

Since CoreWeave’s current P/S is well below its Fair Ratio, this analysis suggests the stock remains undervalued even after its impressive gains earlier this year.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Narrative is a simple but powerful tool that lets you create your own story about a company like CoreWeave by linking your view on its future (such as expected revenue, margins, and fair value) with the numbers behind it.
